sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 67, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 66 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The ACTION7 didn't seem to change anything visible (the image looks the same). The level still isn't complete.
Let me reconsider the entire puzzle. Looking at the bottom panel, it shows Red → Green → Blue as the sequence. All three are highlighted. But the level isn't complete.
Maybe the goal isn't just to collect all blocks, but to arrange them in a specific order on the board. The bottom panel shows the target order: Red, Green, Blue (left to right). Currently on the board, the blocks are stacked vertically: Red (top), Blue (middle), Green (bottom).
Wait - looking more carefully at the bottom panel, the blocks are arranged horizontally: Red, Green, Blue. Maybe the goal is to arrange the blocks horizontally in that order on the board?
Or maybe the goal is to have the blocks in the vertical order matching the bottom panel: Red (top), Green (middle), Blue (bottom)?
Currently the vertical order is Red, Blue, Green. So I need to swap Blue and Green.
Let me think about how to do this:
1. The trail can push/pull blocks horizontally
2. The player can move vertically
3. Blocks stay at their column positions when the player moves vertically
To swap Blue and Green:
- Blue is at rows 19-22, Green is at rows 37-40
- I need to move Blue down and Green up, or move one aside and then the other
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the mechanics. When I extend the trail RIGHT, it pushes blocks to the right. When I retract LEFT, it pulls blocks to the left. The blocks stay at their row positions.
So I can't directly swap the vertical positions of blocks using the trail. The trail only moves blocks horizontally.
Maybe the goal is different. Let me look at the bottom panel again. It shows Red, Green, Blue in that order. All three are highlighted. Maybe the goal is to have the blocks collected in that order, which I've done.
But the level isn't complete. Maybe there's something else I need to do.
Let me try moving the player to different positions and see if the level completes when the player is at a specific location. Or maybe I need to extend the trail in a specific pattern.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to have the blocks arranged in the same horizontal order as the bottom panel. The bottom panel shows Red, Green, Blue from left to right. Currently, all three blocks are at the same column (18-21). Maybe I need to spread them out horizontally in the order Red, Green, Blue?
Let me try:
1. Move to Red's row, extend RIGHT to push Red right
2. Move to Green's row, extend RIGHT to push Green right (but not as far as Red)
3. Move to Blue's row, extend RIGHT to push Blue right (but not as far as Green)
This would create a horizontal arrangement: Red (rightmost), Green (middle), Blue (leftmost)... but that's the reverse order.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The bottom panel shows the collection order, not the target arrangement. Maybe the goal is simply to collect all blocks, and the level completes automatically. But it hasn't completed yet.
Let me try pressing ACTION7 one more time, or try moving the player to the very top of the bar.
Wait, I just noticed something. The player moved UP 6 times and the blocks also moved UP! Looking at the output:
- Before: Red at rows 25-28, Blue at rows 31-34, Green at rows 37-40
- After: Red at rows 13-16, Blue at rows 19-22, Green at rows 37-40
So Red moved from 25-28 to 13-16 (up 12 rows), Blue moved from 31-34 to 19-22 (up 12 rows), but Green stayed at 37-40.
This is interesting! When the player moved UP, the blocks that were "attached" to the player (via the trail or proximity) also moved. But Green didn't move because it wasn't attached.
Wait, but I retracted the trail before moving UP. So how did Red and Blue move?
Let me look at the image again. The player is at the top of the bar, and Red and Blue are next to the player. Green is still at the bottom. So it seems like when I moved UP, the blocks that were adjacent to the player's trail moved with the player.
This suggests that blocks "stick" to the player/trail when they're in contact. So maybe the goal is to collect all blocks and bring them to a specific location (like the top of the bar)?
But Green is still at the bottom. Maybe I need to collect Green and bring it to the top as well?
Let me try moving DOWN to collect Green, then move UP to bring all blocks to the top.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Interesting! When player moved UP, Red and Blue moved with player but Green stayed
# Red: 13-16, Blue: 19-22, Green: 37-40
# Blocks seem to "stick" to player/trail when in contact
# Need to collect Green and bring it up too

# Player at rows ~14-19 (top of bar)
# Green at rows 37-40
# Move DOWN to align with Green, extend RIGHT to touch it, then move UP

# First check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Move DOWN to Green's row (37-40)
# Player currently at ~14-19, need to go to ~36-41
# That's about 22 rows down, or 11 DOWN actions (2 rows each)
action(['DOWN'] * 11)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player: rows 24-29, cols 11-16
Player: rows 56-61, cols 20-25
